segments.s379-g73: بیامد is a past simple (literary) verb meaning “came” (3rd person singular); the subject is omitted and understood from context. به + درگاه is a prepositional phrase “to/into the court/presence”; آزاد شاه is a noun phrase where آزاد (an epithet meaning “noble/ generous”) precedes شاه (king) — a more archaic/poetic word order and the linking ezâfe is not written, so the whole phrase reads “to the court of the noble king.”
segments.s379-g74: This is a coordinated participial description of the subject’s condition as he came. کمر بسته is the idiom ‘کمر بستن’ (to gird one’s loins) where کمر (waist/belt) + بسته (past participle/adjective, “girded/tied”) describes readiness for action. و links that to بر نهاده کلاه, where بر (on/upon) + نهاده (past participle of نهادن, “placed/put”) functions as a perfective participle meaning “having put on” (i.e. ‘with a hat placed on [his head]’); کلاه is the object “hat/helmet.” The whole phrase modifies the omitted subject of بیامد.
segments.s379-g33: همی (still) + تا بدین (until/to this point) + اندرون (inside) + بود (was) + شاه (the king). Word order is literary: the adverb همی and the temporal preposition تا بدین set the time (‘still/until now’), اندرون is the locative complement, and بود شاه is the copular clause ‘the king was (there).’ Note that بدین here is a fused poetic form meaning ‘to this/until now.’
segments.s379-g34: پدید آمد (appeared/came into view) + از دور (from afar) + گرد سیاه (a black گرد). پدید آمد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning ‘came into sight’; از دور gives source/distance. گرد سیاه is a compact noun phrase: گرد here denotes a throng/host (poetic usage) modified by سیاه ‘black’ — together ‘a black host/column.’
segments.s379-g35: چراغ جهان بود is a nominal clause: چراغ جهان ‘lamp/guide of the world’ with بود ‘was’ — a praise formula meaning ‘he was the light of the world.’ دستور شاه follows as an appositive title; in poetic usage دستور functions as ‘commander/chief’ (not merely ‘order’).
segments.s379-g36: فرستاده شاه زی پور شاه lists roles and a name: فرستاده ‘the envoy/messenger’ + شاه (the king’s) — then the personal/name element زی پور شاه (a compound name/title). زی here functions as a connective in names (‘of/from’ element) and پور is the name-element ‘-pur/son’ — together forming the title/name 'Zīpur Shah.' The phrase identifies him as the king’s envoy.
segments.s379-g37: چو (when) + از دور (from afar) + دیدش (he saw them / he saw him; -ش is object clitic) + ز کهسار گرد (a throng/from the upland). The clause is temporal: چو introduces the time clause. دیدش places the object immediately on the verb (archaic word order). ز کهسار indicates origin ‘from the mountain/upland’; گرد again denotes the group/throng seen.
segments.s379-g38: بدانست (he knew it) is the main verb of cognition, followed by the content clause کامد فرستاده مرد. کامد is an archaic/poetic present/near-future form ‘comes/has come,’ and فرستاده مرد ‘the messenger-man’ is the subject of that embedded idea. Together: ‘he knew (that) the messenger had come/was coming.’
segments.s379-g39: پذیره شدش is a passive/experiential construction ‘he was received/welcomed’ (شدش = ‘it happened to him / he became’ with پذیره = reception). گرد فرزند شاه functions as the agent/party doing the receiving — ‘by the prince’s band/party’ (گرد used poetically for a group). Word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s379-g40: همی بود تا او بیامد ز راه: همی بود ‘they/it remained’ or ‘there it was’ (continuous past), تا introduces the temporal limit ‘until,’ او بیامد ‘he came/arrived’ (بیامد = came), ز راه ‘from/by the road.’ The clause means ‘they remained until he arrived by the road.’
segments.s379-g1: بدان روزگار is a time adverbial meaning “in/at that time.” Here the apparent split ب + دان functions together as the demonstrative/adverbial (older poetic spelling), followed by روزگار “time.” اندر is the preposition “in/within,” and اسفندیار is the personal name (subject). Word order is topical: the time phrase comes first, then the location and person.
segments.s379-g2: به دشت اندرون is a locative phrase: به (towards/into) + دشت “plain” + اندرون “inner/within,” giving “to/into the inner plain” or more loosely “on the inner plain.” بد ز بهر شکار functions as a purpose phrase: ز بهر (for the sake of) + شکار “hunt”; بد is an archaic/poetic element here whose exact grammatical role is uncertain in isolation (see note). The whole segment describes Esfandiyar’s presence on the plain for hunting.
segments.s379-g3: ازان دشت آواز کردش کسی = “From that plain someone called out to him.” ازان (از + آن) is a poetic fused form meaning “from that/there.” آواز کردن is the verbal phrase “to call/shout”; here کردش is کرد + enclitic -ش (= “him”), so the verb is past tense ‘called (to) him.’ The clause sets the scene for reported speech or information.
segments.s379-g4: که introduces the content of the call/what was said (“that/which”); جاماسپ را is the definite object marked by را. کرد is the past tense of کردن “to make/do,” here “made,” خسرو is used as a common noun meaning “king/sovereign,” and گسی is an indefinite modifier “a certain/one.” The clause therefore reports that a certain person made Jamasp king.
segments.s379-g5: چو آن بانگ بشنید آمد شگفت = “When he heard that cry, he came amazed.” چو is the temporal conjunction “when”; آن بانگ is “that cry,” بشنید is the simple past “he heard,” آمد is “he came,” and شگفت is an adjectival/resultative “astonished/astonishment.” This is a sequence of past actions with the cause (hearing) leading to his coming in astonishment.
segments.s379-g6: بپیچید و خندیدن اندر گرفت = “he turned about and began to laugh.” بپیچید is the past of پیچیدن with the prefix ب- (here a simple past/preterite meaning “he turned/twisted”), و is the coordinating conjunction “and,” خندیدن is the verbal noun/infinitive “laughing,” and اندر گرفت is a common periphrasis where گرفتن after a verbal noun means “to begin to [verb]” or “to seize with [the action].” The whole describes a sudden physical reaction followed by laughter.
segments.s379-g7: پسر بود او را گزیده چهار is a somewhat inverted/past construction meaning “he had four chosen sons.” پسر “son” is the noun, بود is the past of بودن (here ‘there was/ he had’), او را is the dative/possessive-like construction “for him / he had,” and گزیده (literally “chosen”) modifies the following numeral چهار “four.” The word order is older/poetic: predicate (پسر) + copula + possessed phrase + modifier.
segments.s379-g8: همه رزم‌جوی و همه نیزه‌دار lists qualities of those sons: همه “all,” رزم‌جوی “war-seeking/warrior,” و “and,” followed again by همه “all,” نیزه‌دار “spear‑bearers/spearmen.” The parallelism (همه ... و همه ...) emphasizes that each of the four sons shared both attributes.
segments.s379-g25: سر شهریارانش is a possessive noun phrase: سر 'head/chief' + شهریارانش 'his nobles/lords' (ش is the possessive suffix). گفت is the verb 'said' (past) that introduces direct speech; the subject is implicit and supplied by the verb ending. ای پسر is the vocative interjection addressing 'O son'. Word order is topic/place phrase → verb → direct address in this poetic style.
segments.s379-g26: ندانم is the 1st-person negative present/past-report form 'I do not know / I know not' with the enclitic -م. گناهی is an indefinite direct object 'any sin' (ی marks indefiniteness). به جای پدر is a prepositional phrase 'in place of / instead of (the) father' where به functions as the preposition and جای = 'place/instead'. The whole clause supplies the content of the speech that began in the previous segment.
segments.s379-g27: مگر آنک functions as a concessive/contrastive connector 'except that / save that' (آنک = 'that'). تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until', followed by دین 'the faith' as its object and بیاموختم as the first-person past/past-perfective 'I learned' (archaic form). The clause sets the temporal boundary for the action in the next segment.
segments.s379-g28: همی is a poetic/adverbial particle indicating habitual or continuous action ('continually / always'); در جهان is the locative prepositional phrase 'in the world'; آتش is the direct object 'fire'; افروختم is the 1st-person past 'I kindled' (archaic form). Word order here is adverb → locative → object → verb, typical of elevated/poetic diction.
segments.s379-g29: جهان ویژه کردم is a transitive clause 'I made the world special/dedicated' where کردم is the 1st-person past of 'do/make' and ویژه here means 'dedicated/assigned'. به introduces the beneficiary/recipient phrase 'to the sharp sword' (به + برنده تیغ), with برنده functioning adjectivally 'cutting/sharp' modifying تیغ 'sword'. The subject 'I' is encoded in the verb ending.
segments.s379-g30: This is an interrogative clause beginning with چرا 'why'. دارد is the verb 'has/bears' (3rd-person present/past-narrative sense here), followed by از من 'toward/from me' giving the reference, then دل شاه میغ ('the king's heart [has] a blade') where میغ 'blade' is used metaphorically as a predicate/attribute of دل. The poetic order places the predicate cluster after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s379-g31: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily / truly'. دلش is the noun + possessive suffix 'his heart' (دل + ش). دیو is the agent 'a demon', placed before the verb for emphasis. بفریفتست is an archaic perfective/past 'has deceived' (بـ- prefix + old past ending -ست), so the clause reads 'a demon has deceived his heart.' The verb form and word order are characteristic of epic/poetic Persian.
segments.s379-g32: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'who/which/that'. بر marks direction/target 'toward' and with کشتن 'killing' forms بر کشتن 'toward killing' (i.e. bent on killing). من is the object 'me'. بیاشیفتست is an archaic perfective/preterite 3rd-person meaning 'has inclined / is inclined' or 'is bent'—so the clause modifies دیو, 'who is bent on killing me.'
segments.s379-g57: خردمند is a noun subject 'the wise (man)'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' introducing direct speech; Persian often puts speaker + گفت before the quoted words. ای is the vocative particle 'O' used to address someone, and شه (an archaic form of شاه) + پهلوان are epithets addressing the addressee ('king' + 'champion').
segments.s379-g58: This is a verbless descriptive clause: به + دانندگی ('in/with knowledge') + پیر ('old') states a quality (elliptical 'you are'). و links the parallel phrase بختت جوان where بخت-ت is 'your fortune' and جوان is the predicate 'young'. In Persian poetry the copula («هستی/است») is often omitted, so both predicates attach to the addressed person.
segments.s379-g59: تو دانی is present-second-person 'you know' (دانی is the familiar/poetic form of 'you know'). که introduces a subordinate content clause. خشم پدر بر پسر is a noun phrase 'the father's anger against/toward the son' with بر marking the target of anger.
segments.s379-g60: به از here forms a comparative idiom 'better than' (the verb/copula is omitted). جور is the noun 'injustice/oppression'. مهتر پسر is 'the elder/older son' (مهتر = elder), and بر پدر marks the object/target 'against the father'. The whole phrase is a nominal comparison: '... (is) better than the injustice of the elder son toward his father.'
segments.s379-g61: ببایدت is an older/poetic form meaning 'it is necessary for you' (باید + enclitic ـت). رفتن is the infinitive 'to go' (forming the necessity 'you must go'). چنینست 'it is thus' (چنین + است contracted), followed by روی used figuratively as 'way/face/manner' — together: 'such is the way/appearance.'
segments.s379-g62: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause. هرچ = 'whatever/whatever it is that', او کند = 'he does' (او subject + کند present/optative form meaning 'does'), پادشاهست is an archaic fused copula 'is king' (پادشاه + -ست), and اوی is an emphatic/old third-person pronoun reinforcing 'he' (literally 'he is he'). The clause gives the reason: 'for whatever he does — he is king.'
segments.s379-g63: برین is the contracted poetic spelling of بر این ('upon this' / 'thereupon'). بر نهادند is past-tense plural 'they set/placed (it/on it)' (نهادند = they placed). و گشتند باز is 'and they turned back/returned' (گشتند = they turned/returned, باز = back/again). The verbs are past plural, describing the actors' actions.
segments.s379-g64: A nominative series: فرستاده 'the envoy/messenger' and پور خسرو 'Khosrow's son' followed by نیاز, which here functions as a proper name (Niyāz) or an epithet. In context the phrase names those who return or are sent back. The coordinating و simply links the two persons.
segments.s379-g9: بند is an introductory indefinite: 'یکی' here works like 'one / a certain (person)' introducing a named individual. 'نام' is the predicate noun 'name'. 'بهمن دوم' is an ordinal personal name, 'Bahman the Second' (the adjective دوم follows the name). 'مهرنوش' is a proper name apposed to or identifying that same person. The order is elliptical/poetic: literally 'one, (whose) name (was) Bahman the Second — Mehrnush.'
segments.s379-g10: This clause continues the catalogue of names. 'نام او' = 'his/its name' (او coreferential); the following element is an epithet/secondary name. In classical poetic style the copula 'was' is often omitted or compressed; here the sequence intends 'another (one), (his) name (was) ...'. 'بد' in this position is not being used as the ordinary adjective 'bad' but functions archaically as part of a predicate construction (a contracted/copular function) or an emphatic particle; the following 'دلافروز' is a laudatory compound name 'delāfarūz' (heart‑brightening), and 'طوش' is a short attributive element (here part of the name/epithet).
segments.s379-g11: چهارم is the ordinal 'the fourth'. The sequence 'بدش' should be read as a contracted/archaic verbal phrase meaning 'he gave to him / he named him' with the enclitic ش = 'him / her / it' marking the indirect object; thus 'چهارم بدش نام نوشاذرا' = 'he gave the fourth the name Nushâdra' (i.e. 'he named the fourth Nushâdra'). 'نام نوشاذرا' is a direct object phrase, 'the name Nushâdra.'
segments.s379-g12: نهادی here is the 2nd‑person past of نهادن 'to place/put' (classical form), addressing someone: 'you placed / did you place'. کجا = 'where'. گنبد in epic diction often denotes the vaulted sky or a dome. آذرا is a proper name (addressed vocatively or as possessor). The clause is a tersely posed question: 'Where did you place the dome, Azra?'
segments.s379-g13: The line begins with the indirect object marked by the preposition 'به' — 'to the king of the world' (به شاه جهان) — followed by گفت 'said'. Poetic word order places the speaker after the verb: 'بهمن پسر' ('Bahman, the son') is the subject, so the natural reading is 'Bahman the son said to the king of the world.'
segments.s379-g14: This is an optative/wish clause introduced by 'که' (here functioning 'that / may it be that'). 'تا' helps form the durative 'forever' sense together with 'جاودان' (eternal). 'سبز' (= 'green') is used idiomatically of the head as a blessing meaning 'long‑lived / flourishing.' 'بادات' is the optative form from باد (here 'may it be'), and 'سر' is the noun 'head' (the thing being blessed); literal order places the verb/optative before the head. Together: 'May your head remain green eternally.'
segments.s379-g15: یکی here functions adverbially/indefinitely ('once' / 'one time' / 'at that moment'). 'ژرف خنده' is a compound noun phrase 'a deep (ژرف) laugh (خنده)'. 'بخندید' is the 3rd person past 'he laughed' (the b‑ prefix is the classical verbal form), and 'شاه' (the king) appears after the verb by poetic inversion: 'the king laughed a deep laugh.'
segments.s379-g16: نیابم is negative + verb from یابیدن 'to find' meaning 'I cannot find / I do not find' (classical negative formation with initial ن). 'همی' is an emphatic/adverbial particle intensifying the negation ('at all / indeed'), 'اندرین' = 'in this / in these (matters)', 'هیچ' = 'any / any (at all)' and 'راه' = 'way / means'. The clause thus reads 'I can find no way in this' (i.e. 'I see no solution here').
segments.s379-g65: This is a past narrative clause: 'یکی' here functions like an indefinite/article 'a' or 'a certain', followed by 'جای خوبش' ('a good place of his' = 'a good place for him'). 'فرود آورید' is the classical/poetic past action 'they put/set down' (lit. 'brought down'), where the compound 'فرود آوردن' means 'to make alight / to set down'. Word order is Subject omitted + object phrase + verb (common in Persian narrative).
segments.s379-g66: 'به کف بر گرفتند' is a single verbal phrase meaning 'they took up into the palm' or 'they lifted into their hands': 'به' here marks direction/toward, 'کف' = 'palm', and 'بر' functions as an up‑prefix with the verb 'گرفتند' ('they took' / past 3pl). 'هر دو نبید' is the object 'both wines' (literally 'each/both the two wines'). Word order places the directional phrase before the verb and then the object.
segments.s379-g67: 'به پیشش' = 'in front of him / before him' (prepositional phrase). 'همی' is the archaic/progressive particle indicating continuous or habitual action ('kept on / was continuously'), and 'عود می‌سوختند' is an imperfective past construction: the verb 'می‌سوختند' (they were burning) describes that agarwood (عود) was burning repeatedly or continuously in his presence.
segments.s379-g68: Literally 'you said' (تو گفتی) in simple past 2nd‑person; here it functions as an interjectional remark before the clause 'همی آتش افروختند' ('they kept kindling the fire'). 'همی' again marks repeated/continuous action and 'افروختند' is past 3rd‑plural 'they kindled'. Note: in the edition given the lexical senses for 'گفتی' do not include 'said', so that specific sense is not in the supplied sense list.
segments.s379-g69: 'دگر روز' = 'the next/another day' (classical narrative phrase). 'بنشست' is past 3rd‑person singular 'he sat (down)' and is followed by the locative 'بر تخت خویش' ('upon his throne') — note 'خویش' is the possessive 'his/her/one's own' attached to خوی (here functioning as the possessive base), so the whole phrase means 'sat on his own throne.'
segments.s379-g70: 'ز لشکر' uses the poetic preposition 'ز' = 'from', so 'from the army'. 'بیامد' is past 'came' (here 3rd‑person singular), and 'فراوان' is an adverb 'in great numbers/many'. The phrase ends with 'به پیش' = 'before him / forward', so the clause says many came forward from the army into his presence. Word order places the source 'ز لشکر' before the verb.
segments.s379-g71: 'همه لشکرش را' = 'his entire army' with 'را' marking the definite/direct object. 'به بهمن سپرد' is 'he entrusted (it) to Bahman' — 'سپرد' past 3rd‑person 'he handed/entrusted', with 'به' indicating the recipient. Word order is object + recipient + verb, typical in Persian when the object is topicalized.
segments.s379-g72: 'وزانجا' (= 'و ز آن‌جا') = 'and from there'; 'خرامید' is past 'he strode/sauntered proudly' (3rd‑person past). 'با چند گرد' means 'with a few (چند) گردs' — 'با' = 'with' and 'چند' = 'several/a few'. 'گرد' here denotes a troop/unit in context (a classical military term), so the phrase means 'with a small detachment.'
segments.s379-g49: Word order is inverted: the object/topic خردمند را (‘the wise man’, marked with را) comes first, followed by گفتش (‘said to him’), then the subject name اسفندیار. گفتش is گفت + enclitic ش meaning “said to him/her” (the ش refers back to خردمند). This is a topicalizing construction: “To the wise man Esfandiyar said.”
segments.s379-g50: چه is the interrogative 'what/what ...' and introduces the question; بینی is 2nd-person present subjunctive/indicative 'you see/you think' (here meaning 'what do you think'); م + را in the manuscript corresponds to مرا ‘me’ (object pronoun) — the segmenting in the data is nonstandard, so the pronoun functions as the object of بینی; اندرین روی کار is a prepositional phrase 'in/with regard to this matter' (اندرین 'in/this', روی 'aspect/on', کار 'affair/matter').
segments.s379-g51: گر is the conditional 'if'; ایدونک is an archaic/emphatic particle ('if thus' / 'so'); با تو بیایم is the verb phrase 'I come to you' with بیایم in the subjunctive/optative sense 'should I come' or 'I should come'; به در literally 'to the door/gate' here = 'to your presence/court'. The clause sets up a hypothetical condition.
segments.s379-g52: نه is negation 'not'; نیکو 'well/good'; کند is 3rd-person singular present/future 'it will do/make' (here impersonal 'will go well'); کار 'the matter/affair'; با من 'for/with me' (shows who benefits); پدر is a vocative 'father' addressing the addressee. Together: 'will the matter not go well with me, father?'.
segments.s379-g53: ور is an alternative conditional 'or / if'; ایدونک again is the archaic particle; نایم here functions as a negative/contrasting verb form 'I do not come' or 'I may not come' (the printed lemma in the data does not reflect the verb form exactly); به فرمانبری 'in obedience/obediently' is the adverbial phrase modifying the verb. The clause offers the alternative condition 'if I do not come obediently'.
segments.s379-g54: برون کرده باشم is a perfect-subjunctive/periphrastic construction: برون کرده (past participle 'having brought/put out') + باشم (subjunctive of بودن) meaning 'I would have brought out' or idiomatically 'I would have lifted/raised (myself)'; سر از کهتری 'head from lowliness' is the noun phrase functioning as the object/complement — idiomatically 'to lift one's head out of (one's) lowliness/abjection'. Word order places the verbal complex before the noun phrase.
segments.s379-g55: یکی here means 'one' / 'someone' / 'a certain (person)'; چاره‌ساز‌ای is an adjectival/nominal phrase 'problem-solver' (the -ای suffix is an archaic/poetic form); خردمند پیر is a stacked modifier 'wise old (man)' — altogether 'a certain wise old problem‑solver'. This is a nominal phrase introducing the subject of the following clause.
segments.s379-g56: نیابد is a negative present/subjunctive form of یافتن 'to find' (here 'one does not find' / 'will not be found'); چنین 'thus/so' modifies the following verb; ماند 'remains' is present 'will remain'; بر خیره 'upon the astonished/onlookers' (بر + خیره); خیر 'goodness/benefit' — the clause says that no such person is found and therefore the situation remains so, with no good for the astonished. Note the compact poetic order (verb before subject-complement).
segments.s379-g41: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. باره here means 'load / baggage' and modifies چمنده (a pack or trunk), so ز باره چمنده = 'from the packed baggage/pack'. فرود آمدند is a literary compound verb (فرود آمدن) in the past/plural: 'they descended / alighted'. The verb comes after its prepositional phrase, a normal word order in classical Persian narration.
segments.s379-g42: گو here functions as a narrative/discourse particle (literary 'said' / 'then'), introducing what follows. پیر is the nominal subject 'the old man'. هر دو is the idiom 'both', and پیاده شدند is the plural past/vestigial copulative/auxiliary construction meaning 'they became on foot' = 'they dismounted / went on foot'. Note the classical order: particle + subject + phrase + finite verb.
segments.s379-g43: بپرسید is a past/formal third‑person verb 'asked'. The syntax is inverted (verb before subject): 'بپرسید ... فرخ اسفندیار' = 'Esfandiyar asked'. ازو is the oblique 'from him' (the person being questioned). فرخ is an epithet 'the Fortunate' attached to the name اسفندیار.
segments.s379-g44: که introduces an indirect question or reported content ('that / what'). چونست is an archaic interrogative/copular form meaning 'how is it / what is he like' (چون + است contracted). شاه آن گو نامدار is the noun phrase 'that famed king' — آن 'that', نامدار 'renowned'; گو here is a literary particle used before adjectives/names (classical usage often '-گو'/'گو' as a so‑called/poetic linker), so the whole phrase asks 'how is that so‑renowned king?'.
segments.s379-g45: خردمند is the nominative noun 'the wise man'. گفتا is the archaic past form 'said' used frequently in epic narrative. درستست is a verbless predication with the copular -ست ('it is correct / that is right'). و شاد links the clause with 'and (he was) glad' — here شاد is an adjectival predicate describing his emotion.
segments.s379-g46: برش را is the definite object (را marks the object) — contextually 'him' / 'his person' (the exact body‑part/term is elliptical in the text) and is what is kissed. ببوسید is the past tense 'he kissed'. و links the next action: نامه بداد is 'he gave the letter' — بداد here appears as the past of دادن in the classical narrative sense 'gave' (the lemma senses list provided is mismatched, but syntactically this is a simple past transitive: object (نامه) + verb (بداد)).
segments.s379-g47: درست here is an adverb/adjective 'accurately / correctly'. از همه کارش is a prepositional/possessive phrase 'of / concerning all his affairs' (از + همه + کار + ـش). آگاه کرد is a causative/perfect construction: آگاه 'aware' + کرد 'made / did' = 'informed / made (him) aware'. The verb کرد is the main past tense verb governing the whole clause.
segments.s379-g48: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. مر is the oblique pronoun 'to me / of me' (here 'my'), marking perspective: مر شاه را = 'my king' (شاه = king, را = object marker). دیو بی‌راه کرد is 'a demon made (him) go astray' — دیو 'demon', بی‌راه 'off the right path / astray', کرد past 'did / caused'. Together: 'that a demon had misled my king.'
segments.s379-g17: بدو گفت is an inverted sequence meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = به او, گفت = said). پورا is an emphatic/adverbial word ('completely/indeed') modifying the following time expression. بدین روزگار is a frozen locative expression 'in these times' (بدین = 'in/at these', روزگار = 'time/age'), giving the temporal frame for the complaint.
segments.s379-g18: This clause gives the content of what was said. کس آید = 'no one/anyone comes' (کس here used as 'no one' in context). مرا is the dative/accusative 'to me' (here written in the word list oddly as 'م'); را is the object/definiteness marker; از در شهریار = 'from the gate/door of the king' (در here means 'gate/door'). Word order is subject–verb–(object/dative) with a locative complement.
segments.s379-g19: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'for'); آواز بشنیدم is a past action 'I heard a voice' (بشنیدم = I heard). از ناگهان is an adverbial phrase 'suddenly' formed with the preposition از + ناگهان, stressing abruptness.
segments.s379-g20: بترسم is a present/future-fused first-person form meaning 'I fear / I am afraid' (ب‑ prefix marking the imperfective/optative in classical Persian). که introduces the content of the fear; از گفته is 'because of/from the saying' (گفته = 'said / saying' used nominally), and بی‌رهان is an adjective 'unrestrained / loose', so the clause expresses fear that the cause is loose/unconsidered speech.
segments.s379-g21: ز من = 'because of me / on my account' (poetic preposition ز = از). خسرو is the subject (the king). آزار دارد combines a noun 'آزار' (harm/annoyance) with دارد 'has/keeps', yielding 'causes (me) harm' or 'is hostile/harassing'. همی is an archaic aspectual particle adding continuity/iteration: 'continually / always.'
segments.s379-g22: دلش = 'his heart' (دل + possessive ش). از رهی = 'on account of / because of a wayfarer' (رهی = 'wayfarer, traveller'). بار دارد is 'bears a burden / is burdened' (بار = burden here), and the final همی again marks continuous or habitual state ('ever / always'). The clause states the king's heart is perpetually burdened by that wayfarer.
segments.s379-g23: گرانمایه فرزند is a noun phrase 'the precious/esteemed son' (epithet + noun). گفتا is an archaic past form 'said' (classical poetic past), and چرا is the interrogative 'why' used to begin the son's question.
segments.s379-g24: This is the direct interrogative content: چه کردی تو = 'what did you do, you' (چه = what; کردی = you did; تو is emphatic 'you'). با خسرو کشورا uses با ('with' / 'toward') to link خسرو (the king/Khosrow) and کشورا (the country/realm), so the speaker asks what the addressee has done regarding the king and the kingdom.

sentences.s379-s37: He came into the noble king's court, girded at the waist and with a hat placed on (his head).
sentences.s379-s17: The king remained inside until then; from afar a black host appeared.
sentences.s379-s18: He was the lamp of the world — the king’s commander and envoy, Shah Zīpur Shah.
sentences.s379-s19: When from afar he saw them on the mountain, he knew that the messenger had come.
sentences.s379-s20: He was received by the prince’s party, and remained there until he arrived by the road.
sentences.s379-s1: At that time Esfandiyar was out on the inner plain for a hunt.
sentences.s379-s2: From that plain someone called out to him, saying that a certain person had made Jamasp king.
sentences.s379-s3: When he heard that cry he came in astonishment; he turned about and burst into laughter.
sentences.s379-s4: He had four chosen sons, all warriors and all spear-bearers.
sentences.s379-s13: He said to the chief of his nobles, “O son, I do not know of any sin in place of (your) father.”
sentences.s379-s14: Except that, until I learned the faith, I continually kindled a fire in the world.
sentences.s379-s15: I dedicated the world to the sharp sword; why does the king's heart bear a blade against me?
sentences.s379-s16: Truly, his heart has been deceived by a demon, who is bent on killing me.
sentences.s379-s29: The wise man said, “O king and champion: in learning you are old, and in fortune you are young.”
sentences.s379-s30: You know that a father's anger toward his son is better than the injustice of an elder son toward his father.
sentences.s379-s31: You must go; such is the way, for whatever he does, he is king.
sentences.s379-s32: They placed him there and turned back; the envoy and Khosrow's son Niyāz returned.
sentences.s379-s5: One was called Bahman the Second — Mehrnush; another bore the name Sim, his (or its) appellation being Delāfarūz Toush.
sentences.s379-s6: The fourth he named Nushâdra; 'Where, Azra, did you set (place) the dome?'
sentences.s379-s7: Bahman the son said to the king of the world, 'May your head remain green for ever.'
sentences.s379-s8: At that the king laughed a deep laugh; 'I can find no way in this matter.'
sentences.s379-s33: They set him down in a good place, and they took up both cups of wine in their hands.
sentences.s379-s34: Before him agarwood (oud) was burning constantly; as you said, they kept kindling the fire.
sentences.s379-s35: The next day he sat upon his throne; many from the army came forward before him.
sentences.s379-s36: He entrusted his whole army to Bahman, and from there he strode proudly with a few troop‑units.
sentences.s379-s25: Esfandiyar said to the wise man, "What do you see for me in this matter?"
sentences.s379-s26: If, father, I should come to you to the gate like this, will the business not go well for me?
sentences.s379-s27: Or if I do not come in obedience, I shall have raised my head out of lowliness.
sentences.s379-s28: One will not find a single wise old problem‑solver; so the state will remain thus, and no good will come to the astonished.
sentences.s379-s21: They alighted from the loaded pack; then the old man said, "Both dismounted on foot."
sentences.s379-s22: Esfandiyar the Fortunate asked him, "What sort of king is that famed one?"
sentences.s379-s23: The wise man said, "That is correct," and (being pleased) kissed him and gave the letter.
sentences.s379-s24: He accurately informed him of all his affairs, saying that a demon had led my king astray.
sentences.s379-s9: He said to him, “Truly, in these times no one comes to me from the king's gate.”
sentences.s379-s10: For I heard a voice suddenly; I am afraid that it comes from unrestrained talk.
sentences.s379-s11: Because of me Khosrow continually causes me grief; his heart is always burdened because of a wayfarer.
sentences.s379-s12: The esteemed son said, 'Why — what have you done, you, with Khosrow and the country?'
